topic: A person discovers the tree they planted as a child has been cut down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of damp earth and old wood hit me first, a sudden, sharp intrusion into the quiet afternoon. I’d been walking along the edge of the woods, tracing the line where my childhood ambition had taken root. There, standing where a towering oak should have been, was only a jagged scar on the landscape. My breath hitched, catching painfully in my throat. My hands, which had been clasped tightly around my bag, began to tremble violently. I took a hesitant step closer, my eyes darting from the stumps to the empty space where the canopy used to be. A low, involuntary sound escaped my chest, a small, wounded noise. Every instinct screamed at me to run, but my feet felt cemented to the spot. I couldn't move.

[story 2]

It was the silence that was the worst part. A profound, unnatural quiet where the rustling symphony of leaves should have been. I knelt down, my knees protesting against the uneven ground, and ran my fingers over the raw, exposed roots. They were shredded, ragged wounds in the soil. A coldness washed over me, seeping into my bones, making my skin prickle. I pulled my gaze up, scanning the perimeter of the clearing, searching for any sign of movement, any trace of the giant I had nurtured from a sapling. My mouth went dry. I swallowed hard, the movement visible in my neck. My heart hammered against my ribs with such force I thought it might shatter them. I needed to scream, but no sound emerged, only a shallow, ragged gasp that tasted metallic on my tongue.

[story 3]

I stood frozen at the edge of the clearing, the afternoon sun suddenly feeling harsh and unforgiving. The spot where the magnificent maple had stood—the one I had painstakingly watered and protected for years—was now just bare, broken timber. A wave of nausea rolled over me, making my vision swim slightly. I could feel the blood draining from my face, leaving a clammy residue. My hands flew up to grip my arms, knuckles turning white as I fought to keep my limbs from betraying me. I took a stumbling step backward, my gaze locked on the cut base, unable to process the finality of what I was seeing. A desperate urge to flee propelled me forward, but my legs refused to obey, locking me in place as the reality of the devastation settled heavily upon me.
